name: Curd Rice
description: Creamy yogurt mixed with cooked rice, tempered with mustard seeds and curry leaves.
instructions: Mash cooked rice slightly, mix with yogurt, milk, and salt until creamy.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dal, curry leaves, ginger, and green chili; let them splutter.
Pour the tempering over the rice-yogurt mixture and mix well.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes for best flavor, or serve at room temperature.
Garnish with pomegranate seeds before serving.
tips: Add milk to keep the curd rice creamy even after refrigerating.
Best served cold — a classic South Indian comfort food.

name: Dal Tadka
description: Yellow lentils cooked until creamy and finished with a sizzling temper of ghee, garlic, and spices.
instructions: Wash toor dal, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles until soft, then mash well.
Add chopped tomato and salt to the dal, simmer on low for 5 minutes.
In a separate small pan, heat ghee, add cumin seeds, dried red chilies, and sliced garlic; fry until garlic is golden.
Pour the sizzling tadka over the dal, cover immediately to trap the aroma.
Garnish with coriander and serve with steamed rice or chapati.
tips: The tadka must be poured while sizzling hot for the best flavor.
Mash the dal well for a creamy consistency.

name: Dal Makhani
description: Rich and creamy black lentils slow-cooked with butter, cream, and aromatic spices.
instructions: Soak urad dal and rajma overnight, then pressure cook with water for 5-6 whistles until very soft.
In a heavy pan, melt butter, add chopped onion, and sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add tomato puree and spices; cook until oil separates.
Add the cooked dal, mix well, and simmer on very low heat for 30-40 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in cream, adjust salt, and serve garnished with a swirl of cream and a pat of butter.
tips: Low and slow cooking is the secret — never rush dal makhani.
Overnight soaking ensures the lentils cook to a creamy consistency.

name: Chana Masala
description: Chickpeas simmered in a tangy, spiced tomato and onion gravy.
instructions: Heat oil, add finely chopped onions, and sauté until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down.
Add turmeric, red chili powder, chole masala, and salt; cook the masala until oil separates.
Add chickpeas with half a cup of water, mix well, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Finish with amchur powder, garnish with coriander, and serve with bhature or rice.
tips: For deeper flavor, use tea-soaked water when cooking dried chickpeas from scratch.
Mash a few chickpeas against the pan to thicken the gravy.

name: Rajma
description: Punjabi-style kidney beans cooked in a thick, spiced tomato-onion gravy.
instructions: Soak rajma overnight, then pressure cook with water and salt for 6-7 whistles until soft.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add finely chopped onions and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, chopped tomatoes, and all dry spices; cook until the masala is well blended.
Add the cooked rajma with its cooking liquid, simmer for 15-20 minutes until the gravy thickens.
Finish with garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with steamed basmati rice.
tips: Always soak rajma for at least 8 hours for even cooking.
Mashing a few beans helps thicken the curry naturally.

name: Palak Paneer
description: Cottage cheese cubes in a vibrant pureed spinach gravy with garlic and spices.
instructions: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes, immediately transfer to ice water, then blend into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add cumin seeds, garlic, and green chilies; sauté for a minute.
Add chopped onion and tomato; cook until soft.
Add spinach puree, salt, and garam masala; simmer for 5 minutes.
Add paneer cubes, stir in cream, cook for 2-3 minutes, and serve with naan or roti.
tips: Blanching and ice-bathing the spinach preserves its bright green color.
Do not overcook the spinach puree or it will turn dull.

name: Paneer Butter Masala
description: Soft paneer cubes in a rich, creamy tomato-cashew gravy with butter and spices.
instructions: Boil tomatoes and cashews together until soft, cool, and blend into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add ginger-garlic paste, and sauté for a minute.
Add the tomato-cashew puree, red chili powder, salt, and sugar; cook on medium heat for 10 minutes.
Add paneer cubes and cream, simmer for 5 minutes on low heat.
Crush kasuri methi and sprinkle on top, finish with a pat of butter, and serve with naan.
tips: Soaking cashews in warm water for 15 minutes makes blending easier.
Adding sugar balances the acidity of the tomatoes.

name: Aloo Gobi
description: Dry-style potato and cauliflower stir-fry with turmeric, cumin, and coriander.
instructions: Cut potato and cauliflower into similar-sized florets and pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until translucent.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chili powder; cook for 2-3 minutes.
Add potato and cauliflower, toss well, cover, and cook on low heat for 15-18 minutes until tender.
Sprinkle garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti.
tips: Cut vegetables to uniform size for even cooking.
Do not stir too often — let the vegetables develop a light crust.

name: Bhindi Masala
description: Crispy okra stir-fried with onions, tomatoes, and aromatic spices.
instructions: Wash okra, dry thoroughly, and cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil in a wide pan, add cumin seeds and chopped onion; sauté until golden.
Add okra pieces and stir-fry on medium-high heat without covering for 8-10 minutes until crispy.
Add chopped tomato, turmeric, coriander, red chili powder, and salt; cook for 3-4 minutes.
Finish with amchur powder and serve with roti or paratha.
tips: The okra must be completely dry before cutting to prevent sliminess.
Do not cover the pan while cooking okra — it needs dry heat to crisp up.

name: Baingan Bharta
description: Smoky roasted eggplant mash cooked with onions, tomatoes, and green chilies.
instructions: Roast the whole eggplant directly over a gas flame, turning frequently, until the skin is charred and the flesh is soft.
Peel off the charred skin under running water, mash the smoky flesh, and set aside.
Heat mustard oil, add cumin seeds, garlic, and green chilies; sauté briefly.
Add chopped onions and cook until golden, then add tomatoes and spices; cook until oil separates.
Add mashed eggplant, mix well, cook for 5 minutes, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti.
tips: Charring directly over flame gives the authentic smoky flavor — do not skip this step.
Mustard oil is traditional and adds pungent depth.

name: Matar Paneer
description: Cottage cheese and green peas in a mildly spiced tomato-onion gravy.
instructions: Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add pureed tomatoes and all spice powders.
Cook the masala until oil separates, about 5-7 minutes.
Add green peas and half a cup of water; simmer for 8 minutes until peas are tender.
Add paneer cubes, simmer for 3-4 minutes, finish with garam masala, and serve with roti or rice.
tips: Use frozen peas for convenience — they cook perfectly in the gravy.
Do not overcook paneer or it becomes rubbery.

name: Kadai Paneer
description: Paneer and bell peppers cooked in a spicy tomato gravy with freshly ground kadai masala.
instructions: Dry roast coriander seeds and dried red chilies, then coarsely grind into kadai masala.
Heat oil, add chopped onion and garlic; sauté until light golden.
Add pureed tomatoes, salt, and cook until thick and oil separates.
Add the kadai masala, capsicum strips, and julienned ginger; cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add paneer cubes, toss gently, sprinkle kasuri methi, and serve hot with naan.
tips: Freshly ground kadai masala is essential — pre-made spice mixes lack the depth.
Add capsicum late to retain its crunch.

name: Mixed Vegetable Curry
description: A medley of seasonal vegetables simmered in a coconut-tomato gravy.
instructions: Cut all vegetables into uniform bite-sized pieces.
Heat oil, add chopped onion, and sauté until golden; add ginger-garlic paste.
Add chopped tomatoes, turmeric, and red chili powder; cook until masala thickens.
Add all vegetables and one cup of water; cover and cook for 15-18 minutes until tender.
Finish with garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti or rice.
tips: Add harder vegetables like potatoes and carrots first, softer ones like peas later.
Any seasonal vegetables can be substituted based on availability.

name: Sambar
description: South Indian lentil and vegetable stew flavored with tamarind and sambar powder.
instructions: Pressure cook toor dal with turmeric and water until soft, then mash well.
Boil chopped vegetables (drumstick, carrot, onion) separately until tender.
In a pan, heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add tomato, sambar powder, tamarind paste, cooked vegetables, and mashed dal; simmer for 10 minutes.
Adjust salt and consistency, and serve with rice, idli, or dosa.
tips: Use fresh sambar powder for the best flavor.
Adding a small piece of jaggery balances the tamarind sourness.

name: Rasam
description: Tangy South Indian soup made with tamarind, tomatoes, pepper, and cumin.
instructions: Boil chopped tomatoes with tamarind paste, rasam powder, and water until tomatoes are soft.
Add mashed cooked toor dal and salt; bring to a rolling boil.
In a small pan, heat ghee, add mustard seeds, cumin, crushed garlic, and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Pour the tempering into the rasam, garnish with coriander.
Serve hot with steamed rice or drink as a soup.
tips: Rasam should be thin and soupy, not thick like sambar.
Turn off heat as soon as it comes to a frothy boil — do not over-boil.

name: Malai Kofta
description: Deep-fried paneer and potato dumplings served in a rich, creamy tomato-cashew gravy.
instructions: Boil and mash potatoes, crumble paneer, mix with cornstarch and salt; shape into round koftas.
Deep fry koftas on medium heat until golden brown; drain and set aside.
For gravy: blend boiled tomatoes and soaked cashews into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add ginger-garlic paste, pour in the tomato-cashew puree with spices; cook for 10 minutes.
Add cream and kasuri methi, place koftas in the gravy just before serving to keep them crispy.
tips: Add koftas to gravy only while serving — soaking makes them soggy.
If koftas break during frying, add more cornstarch to the mixture.

name: Chole
description: North Indian spiced chickpea curry with a dark, tangy gravy made with tea and spices.
instructions: Soak chickpeas overnight, then pressure cook with a tea bag and water for 5-6 whistles until soft; remove tea bag.
Heat oil, add finely sliced onions, and fry until deep brown for the base.
Add ginger-garlic paste, chopped tomatoes, and all spice powders; cook until oil separates.
Add cooked chickpeas with their dark cooking liquid; simmer for 15 minutes until thick.
Finish with amchur, garnish with ginger juliennes and coriander, serve with bhature or rice.
tips: The tea bag gives chole their characteristic dark color.
Frying onions until very dark brown is essential for authentic flavor.

name: Dal Fry
description: Mixed lentils cooked until creamy, tempered with cumin, garlic, and tomatoes.
instructions: Wash all dals together, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles until soft.
Mash the cooked dal to a smooth consistency.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds and sliced garlic; fry until garlic turns golden.
Add chopped onion, sauté until translucent, then add tomato and red chili powder; cook until soft.
Add the mashed dal, simmer for 5 minutes, adjust salt, and serve with rice or roti.
tips: Using a mix of dals gives a richer texture than a single variety.
A squeeze of lemon at the end brightens the flavor.

name: Lauki Sabzi
description: Mild bottle gourd curry cooked with tomatoes and simple spices.
instructions: Peel and dice bottle gourd into small cubes.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until translucent.
Add tomato, green chili, turmeric, and coriander powder; cook for 2 minutes.
Add lauki cubes, salt, cover, and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes until tender.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti or dal-rice.
tips: Lauki releases a lot of water, so no need to add extra water.
Choose a young, firm lauki for the best texture.

name: Tinda Masala
description: Indian baby round gourd cooked in a simple onion-tomato masala.
instructions: Peel tinda, cut into quarters or halves depending on size.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then chopped onion; sauté until golden.
Add tomato and spice powders; cook until masala thickens.
Add tinda pieces, salt, and a splash of water; cover and cook for 15-18 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti.
tips: Choose tender, small tinda for best results — large ones can be seedy.
Do not add too much water; tinda has high moisture content.

name: Arhar Dal
description: Simple everyday pigeon pea dal tempered with cumin, garlic, and ghee.
instructions: Wash toor dal, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3 whistles until soft.
Mash the dal, add chopped tomato and salt; simmer for 5 minutes.
Heat ghee in a small pan, add cumin seeds, dried red chili, and sliced garlic; fry until golden.
Pour the tadka over the simmering dal, cover for a minute to let flavors meld.
Serve with steamed rice and a side of pickle.
tips: This is a staple everyday dal — keep it simple and let the tadka do the work.
Consistency should be thinner than dal makhani but thicker than rasam.

name: Moong Dal
description: Light and comforting yellow moong dal with a simple garlic and cumin tadka.
instructions: Wash moong dal, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 2-3 whistles until very soft.
Mash lightly with a whisk, add salt, and bring to a simmer.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, sliced garlic, and green chili; fry until golden.
Pour the tadka over the dal, garnish with coriander.
Serve with steamed rice — this is one of the lightest, most digestible dals.
tips: Moong dal cooks quickly and does not need soaking.
Perfect for when you want something light and easy on the stomach.

name: Masoor Dal
description: Quick-cooking red lentil dal with a tomato and onion base.
instructions: Wash masoor dal and pressure cook with turmeric and water for 2 whistles.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, garlic, and chopped onion; sauté until light brown.
Add chopped tomato, red chili powder, and salt; cook until tomato softens.
Add the cooked masoor dal, simmer for 5 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with rice or roti.
tips: Masoor dal cooks the fastest among all dals — do not overcook or it becomes too mushy.
This dal thickens as it cools, so keep it slightly thinner than desired.

name: Cabbage Sabzi
description: Shredded cabbage stir-fried with mustard seeds, turmeric, and green chilies.
instructions: Finely shred the cabbage and chop the onion.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add chopped onion and green chili; sauté for 2 minutes.
Add shredded cabbage, turmeric, and salt; stir-fry on medium-high heat for 5-7 minutes.
The cabbage should be cooked but still have a slight crunch. Squeeze lemon juice and serve.
tips: Do not cover the pan — cabbage should dry-fry, not steam.
Adding grated coconut at the end is a nice South Indian variation.

name: Aloo Matar
description: Potatoes and green peas cooked in a light tomato-based curry.
instructions: Peel and cube potatoes into bite-sized pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, tomato, and spice powders; cook until masala is fragrant.
Add potatoes, peas, salt, and half a cup of water; cover and cook for 15 minutes.
Finish with garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti or puri.
tips: For a richer version, add a splash of cream at the end.
Frozen peas work perfectly and can be added in the last 5 minutes.

name: Paneer Tikka
description: Marinated paneer cubes and vegetables grilled until smoky and charred.
instructions: Cut paneer, capsicum, and onion into 1-inch cubes.
Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, all spice powders, lemon juice, oil, and salt into a smooth marinade.
Coat paneer and vegetables in the marinade; refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill on high heat (or broil in oven) for 8-10 minutes, turning once.
Sprinkle chaat masala and serve with mint chutney and onion rings.
tips: Do not marinate for too long — the acid in yogurt can make paneer crumbly.
A hot grill or oven broiler gives the best char marks.

name: Tandoori Chicken
description: Chicken marinated in yogurt and spices, roasted until smoky with a vibrant red crust.
instructions: Make deep slashes in the chicken pieces for the marinade to penetrate.
Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, tandoori masala, red chili powder, lemon juice, oil, and salt.
Coat chicken thoroughly in the marinade; refrigerate for at least 2 hours (overnight is best).
Bake at 220 degrees Celsius for 25-30 minutes, or grill until charred and juices run clear.
Sprinkle chaat masala, squeeze lemon, and serve with naan and onion rings.
tips: Deep slashes are essential for the marinade to reach the bone.
For smoky flavor at home, place a hot coal in a small bowl of oil inside the marinating container for 2 minutes.

name: Butter Chicken
description: Tender chicken pieces in a silky, buttery tomato-cream sauce with aromatic spices.
instructions: Marinate chicken in yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, red chili powder, and salt for 1 hour.
Grill or pan-sear marinated chicken until lightly charred; set aside.
For the sauce: melt butter, add tomato puree, soaked cashew paste, and spices; cook for 10 minutes.
Strain the sauce for smoothness, add cream, sugar, and the cooked chicken pieces; simmer 10 minutes.
Crush kasuri methi and sprinkle on top, finish with a swirl of cream, and serve with naan.
tips: Straining the sauce gives the characteristic silky-smooth texture.
Sugar is essential to balance the tomato acidity.

name: Chicken Curry
description: Home-style Indian chicken curry with onion-tomato gravy and warm spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add sliced onions, and cook until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add chopped tomatoes and all spice powders.
Cook the masala until oil separates — this is the key to a rich gravy.
Add chicken pieces, coat with the masala, add half a cup of water, cover, and cook for 20-25 minutes.
Finish with garam masala and coriander; serve with rice or roti.
tips: Bone-in chicken gives far more flavor than boneless.
Browning the onions deeply is what makes or breaks this curry.

name: Fish Curry
description: Tender fish pieces simmered in a tangy coconut and tomato curry with curry leaves.
instructions: Rub fish pieces with turmeric and salt; let sit for 10 minutes.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add chopped onion, cook until soft, then add ginger-garlic paste and tomatoes.
Add red chili powder, tamarind paste, coconut milk, and water; bring to a gentle simmer.
Gently slide in the fish pieces, cook uncovered for 8-10 minutes until fish is just cooked through.
tips: Do not stir the fish too much — it breaks apart easily.
Coconut oil is traditional and adds authentic coastal flavor.

name: Egg Curry
description: Boiled eggs simmered in a spiced onion-tomato gravy — a quick protein-rich dinner.
instructions: Hard boil eggs for 10 minutes, peel, and make shallow slits for the gravy to penetrate.
Heat oil, add sliced onions, and fry until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, tomatoes, and all spice powders; cook until oil separates.
Add a cup of water, bring to a simmer, then add the boiled eggs.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes, finish with garam masala and coriander; serve with rice or roti.
tips: Lightly frying the boiled eggs before adding to gravy adds color and texture.
The longer eggs sit in the gravy, the more flavorful they become.

name: Keema
description: Spiced minced meat curry cooked with peas, onions, and aromatic spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add finely chopped onions and cook until deep golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add minced meat, break up any lumps, and cook on high heat until browned.
Add tomatoes, spice powders, salt, and peas; cover and cook for 15-20 minutes until meat is tender.
Finish with garam masala and coriander; serve with roti, naan, or pav.
tips: Brown the mince on high heat without stirring initially for better flavor.
Keema thickens as it rests — it is even better the next day.

name: Vegetable Biryani
description: Layered fragrant basmati rice and spiced mixed vegetables cooked in the dum style.

ingredients.name: whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon)

instructions: Soak rice for 30 minutes, then parboil in salted water with whole spices until 70% cooked; drain.
Slice onions thin and deep fry until crispy and golden (birista); set aside.
Cook vegetables with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, biryani masala, and salt until half-done.
Layer: vegetable masala on the bottom, then rice, fried onions, mint, coriander, and saffron milk; repeat.
Seal the pot with dough or foil, cook on very low heat (dum) for 25 minutes; gently mix while serving.
tips: Parboiling rice to exactly 70% is critical — it finishes cooking during dum.
Sealing the pot tightly traps steam for the signature dum flavor.

name: Chicken Biryani
description: Aromatic layered rice and spiced chicken cooked slowly in sealed pot (dum style).

ingredients.name: whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise)

instructions: Marinate chicken with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, biryani masala, and salt for at least 1 hour.
Soak rice for 30 minutes, parboil with whole spices until 70% done; drain.
Fry thinly sliced onions in ghee until deeply golden; remove half for garnish.
Cook marinated chicken with the remaining fried onions for 10 minutes until half-done.
Layer: chicken on the bottom, then rice, fried onions, mint, coriander, and saffron milk; seal and cook on very low heat for 25-30 minutes.
tips: Never skip marination — it tenderizes the chicken and builds flavor.
The sealed dum cooking is where the magic happens — patience is key.

name: Dal Khichdi
description: Comforting one-pot meal of rice and lentils cooked together with mild spices and ghee.
instructions: Wash rice and moong dal together until water runs clear.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, chopped onion, green chili, and tomato; sauté for 2-3 minutes.
Add rice, dal, turmeric, salt, and water; bring to a boil.
Pressure cook for 3 whistles or simmer covered for 20 minutes until soft and porridge-like.
Serve with a generous dollop of ghee, papad, and pickle.
tips: Khichdi should be soft and mushy — add more water if needed.
This is the ultimate Indian comfort food, often served when unwell.

name: Cucumber Raita
description: Cool yogurt dip with grated cucumber, cumin, and fresh mint.
instructions: Grate cucumber and squeeze out excess water.
Whisk yogurt until smooth, add grated cucumber.
Mix in cumin powder, chopped mint, salt, and a pinch of red chili powder.
Refrigerate for 15 minutes before serving for best flavor.
tips: Squeezing out cucumber water prevents a watery raita.
Roasted cumin powder adds a deeper, nuttier flavor.

name: Boondi Raita
description: Creamy yogurt mixed with crispy gram flour droplets and spiced with cumin.
instructions: Soak boondi in water for 5 minutes until slightly soft, then squeeze out water.
Whisk yogurt until smooth and creamy.
Add softened boondi, cumin powder, salt, and chili powder; mix gently.
Garnish with coriander and serve chilled alongside biryani or pulao.
tips: Use plain boondi, not the masala variety, for raita.
Do not over-soak boondi or it becomes too mushy.

name: Jeera Rice
description: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with cumin seeds and ghee.
instructions: Wash and soak basmati rice for 20 minutes, then drain.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin seeds and bay leaf; let them splutter.
Add drained rice and sauté gently for 1-2 minutes.
Add water and salt, bring to a boil, then cover and cook on low heat for 12-14 minutes.
Fluff with a fork and serve with any dal or curry.
tips: Soaking rice ensures long, separate grains.
Do not stir the rice once water is added — it breaks the grains.

name: Plain Chapati
description: Thin whole wheat flatbread cooked on a hot griddle — the staple Indian bread.
instructions: Mix flour, salt, and water; knead into a soft, smooth dough for 5 minutes.
Rest the dough covered for 10 minutes.
Divide into small balls, dust with flour, and roll into thin circles.
Cook on a hot tawa until bubbles appear, flip, and cook until brown spots appear; it should puff up.
Apply a thin layer of ghee and serve immediately.
tips: Soft dough and thorough kneading give the softest chapatis.
Direct flame contact for the last few seconds helps chapati puff fully.

name: Naan
description: Soft, leavened Indian flatbread baked until pillowy with charred spots.
instructions: Mix warm milk, sugar, and yeast; let sit for 5 minutes until frothy.
Combine flour, baking powder, salt, yogurt, oil, and yeast mixture; knead into a soft dough.
Cover and let rest for 1 hour until doubled in size.
Divide into balls, roll into oval shapes, and cook on a very hot tawa or cast iron pan until charred and puffed.
Brush with garlic butter (minced garlic mixed with melted butter) and serve hot.
tips: A very hot surface is key — naan should char slightly for authentic flavor.
For tandoori effect at home, use a cast iron pan or place under a broiler.

name: Plain Steamed Rice
description: Simple steamed basmati rice — the foundation of an Indian meal.
instructions: Wash rice in several changes of water until water runs clear.
Soak for 20 minutes, then drain.
Add rice, fresh water, and salt to a pot; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to lowest, cover tightly, and cook for 12 minutes.
Turn off heat, keep covered for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.
tips: The 1:2 rice-to-water ratio works for most basmati.
Never stir rice while cooking — it makes it gummy.

name: Vegetable Pulao
description: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with mixed vegetables and whole spices.
instructions: Wash and soak rice for 20 minutes; drain.
Heat ghee, add whole spices (bay leaf, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves), and sauté for a minute.
Add sliced onion and cook until translucent; add chopped vegetables and stir-fry for 2 minutes.
Add drained rice, salt, and water; bring to a boil, then cover and cook on low heat for 14 minutes.
Fluff gently with a fork and serve with raita.
tips: Handle the cooked pulao gently — vigorous stirring breaks the rice grains.
Toasting whole spices in ghee before adding rice adds tremendous fragrance.

name: Lemon Rice
description: South Indian tangy rice tempered with peanuts, mustard seeds, and lemon juice.
instructions: Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts; fry until dals are golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilies, and turmeric; sauté briefly.
Add cooked rice (preferably cooled), salt, and toss gently with the tempering.
Squeeze lemon juice over the rice, mix well, and serve.
Best served at room temperature — perfect for lunchboxes.
tips: Use cold or leftover rice — fresh hot rice can become mushy.
Add lemon juice after turning off heat to preserve its fresh flavor.

name: Plain Yogurt (Dahi)
description: Fresh homemade yogurt set overnight — a staple accompaniment to Indian meals.
instructions: Boil milk and let it cool to lukewarm (about 40 degrees Celsius — you should be able to hold your finger in it comfortably).
Mix in the yogurt starter thoroughly.
Pour into a container, cover, and keep in a warm place undisturbed for 6-8 hours.
Once set, refrigerate to stop fermentation.
Serve chilled as a side with any Indian meal.
tips: Temperature is critical — too hot kills the cultures, too cold prevents setting.
In cold weather, wrap the container in a towel or place in the oven with the light on.

name: Roasted Papad
description: Thin lentil wafer roasted until crispy — the simplest Indian side.
instructions: Hold a papad over a direct gas flame using tongs, rotating constantly.
The papad will puff up and develop charred spots within 15-20 seconds per side.
Alternatively, microwave for 30-40 seconds until crisp.
Serve immediately as a crunchy accompaniment to any meal.
tips: Keep the papad moving constantly over the flame to avoid burning.
Microwave method is easier but lacks the smoky flavor.

name: Aloo Paratha (Side)
description: Stuffed potato flatbread served as a hearty side with curd and pickle.
instructions: Knead flour, water, and salt into a soft dough; rest for 10 minutes.
Boil, peel, and mash potatoes; mix with green chili, cumin, amchur, and salt.
Roll a dough ball, stuff with potato filling, seal, and roll out carefully.
Cook on a hot tawa with ghee on both sides until golden.
Serve with a dollop of butter, yogurt, and pickle.
tips: Dust generously with flour while rolling to prevent sticking.
Press gently while cooking to ensure even browning.

name: Methi Thepla
description: Gujarati spiced fenugreek flatbread — perfect for travel and lunchboxes.
instructions: Wash and finely chop methi leaves; sprinkle with salt and let sit for 5 minutes.
Mix both flours, methi, yogurt, turmeric, chili powder, 1 tbsp oil, and salt; knead into a soft dough using water.
Divide into small balls and roll into thin circles.
Cook on a hot tawa with oil on both sides until golden spots appear.
Stack and wrap in foil — theplas stay fresh for days.
tips: Adding a bit of oil to the dough keeps theplas soft even when cold.
These are the quintessential Gujarati travel food.

name: Puri
description: Deep-fried puffed whole wheat bread — a festive Indian staple.
instructions: Mix flour, salt, and 1 tsp oil; knead with water into a stiff dough (stiffer than chapati dough).
Rest for 10 minutes, then divide into small balls.
Roll each ball into a small, thick circle (about 4 inches diameter).
Heat oil until very hot, slide in a puri, and press gently with a slotted spoon until it puffs up.
Flip once, drain on paper towels, and serve immediately with aloo sabzi or halwa.
tips: The dough must be stiff for puris to puff — soft dough absorbs too much oil.
Oil must be very hot — a small piece of dough should immediately rise to the surface.

name: Mixed Vegetable Raita
description: Yogurt dip with finely chopped mixed vegetables, seasoned with roasted cumin.
instructions: Finely dice cucumber, tomato, onion, and carrot.
Whisk yogurt until smooth.
Mix in all chopped vegetables, cumin powder, chaat masala, and salt.
Garnish with coriander and chill before serving.
tips: Deseed the tomato to prevent excess liquid.
This raita pairs especially well with biryani and pulao.

name: Mango Pickle (Aam ka Achaar)
description: Tangy and spicy raw mango pickle with mustard oil and traditional spices.
instructions: Wash and dry raw mangoes completely; cut into small pieces with the seed.
Mix mango pieces with salt, turmeric, and all spice powders; toss well.
Heat mustard oil until it smokes, let it cool completely.
Pour cooled oil over the spiced mango mixture and mix thoroughly.
Transfer to a clean glass jar, press down, keep in sunlight for 3-4 days, shaking daily.
tips: Mangoes and all utensils must be completely dry to prevent mold.
The pickle improves with age — best after a week of maturing.

name: Samosa
description: Crispy deep-fried pastry filled with spiced potatoes and peas.
instructions: Make a stiff dough with flour, oil, salt, and water; rest for 20 minutes.
Boil and coarsely mash potatoes; mix with peas, cumin, coriander, garam masala, amchur, green chili, and salt.
Divide dough into balls, roll into ovals, cut in half, and shape each half into a cone.
Fill cones with potato mixture, seal edges with water.
Deep fry on medium-low heat (not too hot) until golden and crispy; serve with tamarind and mint chutney.
tips: Fry on medium-low heat for crispy layers — high heat cooks the outside but leaves dough raw inside.
The dough must be stiff for flaky, layered samosas.

name: Pakora
description: Crispy gram flour fritters made with mixed vegetables like onion, spinach, and potato.
instructions: Thinly slice onion and potato; chop spinach and green chilies.
Mix besan, cumin, red chili powder, baking soda, and salt; add vegetables and minimal water to make a thick batter.
Heat oil to medium-hot. Drop spoonfuls of batter into the oil.
Fry in batches until deep golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes per batch.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with mint chutney or ketchup.
tips: Use minimal water — the moisture from vegetables should form most of the batter.
A pinch of baking soda makes pakoras extra crispy.

name: Dhokla
description: Steamed savory gram flour cake from Gujarat, spongy and tangy with a mustard seed tempering.
instructions: Mix besan, yogurt, water, turmeric, salt, and sugar into a smooth batter; let rest for 10 minutes.
Add eno fruit salt (or baking soda + lemon juice), mix quickly as it froths, and immediately pour into a greased steaming plate.
Steam for 12-15 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.
For tempering: heat oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chilies, and a splash of water with sugar.
Pour tempering over the steamed dhokla, cut into squares, and serve with green chutney.
tips: Pour batter immediately after adding eno — the reaction is what makes dhokla spongy.
Do not open the steamer during cooking or dhokla will collapse.

name: Vada Pav
description: Mumbai's iconic street food — spiced potato fritter in a soft bun with chutneys.
instructions: Boil, peel, and mash potatoes. Make a paste of garlic and green chilies.
Heat 1 tbsp oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, and the garlic-chili paste; sauté briefly.
Add turmeric, mashed potatoes, and salt; mix well and shape into round patties.
Make a batter with besan, turmeric, salt, and water; dip each potato ball and deep fry until golden.
Slit pav, spread dry garlic chutney and green chutney, place the vada inside, and serve hot.
tips: The garlic chutney (dry coconut, garlic, red chili) is what makes vada pav iconic.
Press the pav onto a hot buttered tawa for a toasted version.

name: Pav Bhaji
description: Mashed mixed vegetable curry served with buttered, toasted bread rolls.
instructions: Boil potatoes, cauliflower, and peas until very soft; mash everything together.
Heat butter, add chopped onion and capsicum; sauté until soft.
Add chopped tomatoes, pav bhaji masala, and salt; cook until tomatoes break down.
Add the mashed vegetables, mix well, and cook on medium heat for 10 minutes, mashing continuously.
Slit and toast pav with butter, squeeze lemon over the bhaji, garnish with onion and coriander.
tips: Use a potato masher directly in the pan for the characteristic texture.
Generous butter is non-negotiable for authentic pav bhaji.

name: Aloo Tikki
description: Crispy spiced potato patties, shallow-fried until golden and crunchy.
instructions: Boil, peel, and mash potatoes; mix with mashed peas, grated ginger, green chili, cumin, garam masala, coriander, and salt.
Add breadcrumbs to bind, shape into flat round patties.
Heat oil in a flat pan, place tikkis, and shallow fry on medium heat for 3-4 minutes per side until crispy.
Serve with tamarind chutney, mint chutney, and yogurt.
tips: Chill the patties for 15 minutes before frying — they hold shape better.
Breadcrumbs help absorb moisture and make tikkis crispier.

name: Vegetable Spring Rolls
description: Crispy fried rolls filled with stir-fried cabbage, carrots, and bean sprouts.
instructions: Julienne all vegetables finely; mince garlic.
Heat 1 tbsp oil, sauté garlic, add all vegetables, soy sauce, vinegar, and salt; stir-fry on high heat for 2-3 minutes. Cool completely.
Place filling on a spring roll wrapper, fold sides in, and roll tightly; seal edge with cornstarch slurry.
Deep fry in medium-hot oil until golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes.
Serve with sweet chili sauce or schezwan sauce.
tips: The filling must be completely cool before wrapping — hot filling makes wrappers soggy.
Roll tightly to prevent them from opening during frying.

name: Paneer Tikka (Dry)
description: Skewered paneer cubes and vegetables grilled dry with tandoori spices.
instructions: Cut paneer, capsicum, and onion into 1-inch cubes.
Mix yogurt, tandoori masala, lemon juice, oil, and salt into a marinade.
Coat paneer and vegetables, marinate for 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill, broil, or pan-fry on high heat until charred spots appear.
Sprinkle chaat masala and serve with lemon wedges and mint chutney.
tips: For smokier flavor, brush with a tiny bit of oil during grilling.
Cast iron pan works well as a substitute for a tandoor.

name: Corn Chaat
description: Boiled sweet corn tossed with onion, tomato, lime juice, and chaat spices.
instructions: Boil or steam corn kernels until tender; drain.
Toss hot corn with butter, then add finely chopped onion, tomato, and green chili.
Add lemon juice, chaat masala, and black salt; mix well.
Garnish with coriander and serve immediately while warm.
tips: For a smoky version, char the corn on an open flame before removing kernels.
Frozen corn works perfectly for a quick snack.

name: Bhel Puri
description: Crunchy puffed rice snack tossed with sev, chutneys, onions, and raw mango.
instructions: Finely chop onion, tomato, boiled potato, and coriander.
In a large bowl, combine puffed rice, chopped vegetables, and half the sev.
Add tamarind chutney, green chutney, lemon juice, and chaat masala; toss quickly.
Serve immediately, topped with remaining sev for crunch.
tips: Mix and serve immediately — bhel becomes soggy within minutes.
Adjust chutney quantities to taste — the balance of sweet, sour, and spicy is key.

name: Sev Puri
description: Flat crispy puris topped with potato, onion, chutneys, and sev.
instructions: Finely dice boiled potato, onion, and tomato.
Arrange papdi on a plate, top each with diced potato and onion.
Drizzle tamarind chutney and green chutney over each puri.
Top generously with sev, sprinkle chaat masala and coriander, serve immediately.
tips: Assemble just before serving to keep papdi crispy.
Balance of sweet tamarind and spicy green chutney is what makes sev puri special.

name: Vegetable Cutlet
description: Mixed vegetable patty coated in breadcrumbs and shallow-fried until crispy.
instructions: Boil potato, carrot, peas, beans, and corn until tender; mash everything together.
Mix in garam masala, green chili, coriander, and salt.
Shape into oval patties, coat in breadcrumbs.
Shallow fry on medium heat until golden brown on both sides.
Serve with ketchup or mint chutney.
tips: Squeeze out any excess moisture from vegetables before mixing.
Chilling shaped cutlets for 15 minutes helps them hold together while frying.

name: Mathri
description: Crispy, flaky deep-fried crackers made with flour, ajwain, and ghee.
instructions: Mix flour, semolina, ajwain, pepper, and salt; rub in ghee until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs.
Knead into a stiff dough with water; rest for 15 minutes.
Roll out dough, cut into small circles or diamonds, and prick with a fork.
Deep fry on low-medium heat until golden and crispy, about 4-5 minutes per batch.
Cool completely and store in an airtight container — stays crispy for weeks.
tips: Fry on low heat — mathri needs slow cooking to be crispy through the center.
Pricking with a fork prevents puffing and ensures even crispness.

name: Murukku
description: Crunchy spiral South Indian snack made from rice flour and urad dal flour.
instructions: Mix rice flour, urad dal flour, butter, cumin, sesame seeds, asafoetida, and salt.
Add warm water gradually and knead into a smooth, pliable dough.
Load dough into a murukku press with a star-shaped disc.
Press spirals directly into medium-hot oil, fry until golden and crispy.
Drain on paper towels, cool completely, and store in airtight containers.
tips: The dough must be smooth and crack-free for even spirals.
Fry on medium heat — too hot and the outside burns while inside stays raw.

name: Banana Chips
description: Thin slices of raw banana deep-fried in coconut oil until crispy and golden.
instructions: Peel raw bananas and slice very thinly using a mandoline or sharp knife.
Dissolve turmeric and salt in water; briefly dip banana slices in this mixture.
Heat coconut oil in a deep pan until hot.
Fry banana slices in batches on medium heat until golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes.
Drain on paper towels, cool completely, and store in airtight containers.
tips: Use a mandoline for uniformly thin slices — thickness determines crispness.
Coconut oil is traditional and gives the characteristic Kerala flavor.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce with cumin and paprika.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft, about 5 minutes.
Add minced garlic, cumin, paprika, and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute.
Pour in canned tomatoes, break them up, season with salt, and simmer for 10 minutes until thickened.
Make 4 wells in the sauce, crack an egg into each well, cover, and cook for 5-7 minutes until whites are set.
Garnish with parsley and serve with crusty bread for dipping.
tips: Cover the pan for the eggs to cook evenly from top and bottom.
The yolks should still be runny — do not overcook.

name: Greek Salad
description: Classic Mediterranean salad with cucumber, tomato, olives, red onion, and feta cheese.
instructions: Cut cucumber and tomatoes into large chunks; slice red onion thinly.
Combine vegetables in a large bowl, add olives.
Whisk olive oil, vinegar, oregano, salt, and pepper together.
Pour dressing over vegetables and toss gently.
Top with a slab of feta cheese and serve — traditional Greek salad has the feta on top, not crumbled.
tips: Use ripe, in-season tomatoes for the best flavor.
Do not refrigerate too long — Greek salad is best at room temperature.

name: Caprese Salad
description: Simple Italian salad of fresh mozzarella, ripe tomatoes, and basil with olive oil.
instructions: Slice tomatoes and mozzarella into rounds of equal thickness.
Arrange alternating slices of tomato and mozzarella on a plate.
Tuck basil leaves between the slices.
Drizzle with olive oil and balsamic glaze, season with salt and pepper.
tips: Use the best quality mozzarella and tomatoes you can find — this dish depends entirely on ingredient quality.
Burrata can be substituted for mozzarella for a more luxurious version.

name: Minestrone Soup
description: Hearty Italian vegetable soup with beans, pasta, and seasonal vegetables.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, Italian seasoning, and cook for 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes, vegetable broth, and beans; bring to a boil.
Add diced zucchini and pasta; simmer for 12-15 minutes until pasta is tender.
Season with salt, serve topped with grated parmesan.
tips: Add pasta near the end so it does not overcook and become mushy.
This soup is even better the next day as flavors meld.

name: Ratatouille
description: Classic French Provencal stew of eggplant, zucchini, bell peppers, and tomatoes.
instructions: Dice all vegetables into roughly equal-sized cubes.
Heat olive oil, sauté onion and garlic until soft.
Add bell peppers and cook for 5 minutes, then add eggplant and zucchini.
Add canned tomatoes, thyme, salt, and pepper; cover and simmer for 25-30 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Finish with fresh basil and serve with crusty bread or as a side dish.
tips: Salting eggplant cubes for 15 minutes beforehand removes bitterness.
Each vegetable can also be cooked separately first for more distinct textures.

name: Quiche Lorraine
description: Classic French savory tart with bacon, cheese, and custard in a buttery crust.
instructions: Blind bake the pie crust at 190 degrees Celsius for 12 minutes with pie weights, then remove weights and bake 5 more minutes.
Cook bacon until crispy, chop, and scatter over the baked crust along with grated gruyere.
Whisk eggs, cream, milk, nutmeg, salt, and pepper until smooth.
Pour the custard over the bacon and cheese.
Bake at 175 degrees Celsius for 30-35 minutes until the custard is set but still slightly jiggly in the center.
tips: Blind baking prevents a soggy bottom crust.
The quiche continues to set as it cools — do not overbake.

name: Caesar Salad
description: Crisp romaine lettuce with creamy Caesar dressing, croutons, and parmesan.
instructions: For dressing: mince anchovy and garlic into a paste. Whisk with egg yolk, lemon juice, Dijon, and Worcestershire.
Slowly drizzle in olive oil while whisking to emulsify into a creamy dressing.
Tear or chop romaine into bite-sized pieces, wash and dry thoroughly.
Toss lettuce with dressing, croutons, and shaved parmesan.
Grind fresh black pepper on top and serve immediately.
tips: Dry the lettuce well — wet leaves dilute the dressing.
Homemade croutons from day-old bread are far superior to store-bought.

name: Gazpacho
description: Chilled Spanish tomato soup blended with cucumber, pepper, and sherry vinegar.
instructions: Roughly chop tomatoes, cucumber, and bell pepper.
Soak bread in water briefly, squeeze out excess.
Blend all vegetables with bread, garlic, olive oil, vinegar, and salt until smooth.
Strain through a sieve for silky texture (optional), then chill for at least 1 hour.
Serve cold, drizzled with olive oil and garnished with diced vegetables.
tips: Use the ripest, most flavorful tomatoes available.
Gazpacho tastes best when thoroughly chilled for several hours.

name: Nicoise Salad
description: French composed salad with tuna, green beans, potatoes, eggs, and olives.
instructions: Boil potatoes until tender, about 15 minutes; boil eggs for 7 minutes for jammy yolks; blanch green beans for 3 minutes.
Whisk olive oil, mustard, vinegar, and salt into a vinaigrette.
Arrange lettuce on a platter, then compose halved potatoes, green beans, halved tomatoes, olives, and flaked tuna in sections.
Halve the eggs and place on top.
Drizzle vinaigrette over the salad just before serving.
tips: A Nicoise is composed, not tossed — each ingredient is arranged separately.
Use good quality canned tuna packed in olive oil for best results.

name: Bruschetta
description: Toasted Italian bread topped with diced tomatoes, garlic, basil, and olive oil.
instructions: Dice tomatoes, mix with chopped basil, olive oil, balsamic vinegar, and salt; let sit 10 minutes.
Toast or grill bread slices until golden and crisp.
Rub each toast with a cut garlic clove.
Spoon the tomato mixture over the garlic-rubbed toast and serve immediately.
tips: Let the tomato mixture marinate briefly for the flavors to meld.
Serve immediately after topping — the bread goes soggy quickly.

name: Focaccia Sandwich
description: Thick, herbed Italian focaccia bread filled with mozzarella, roasted vegetables, and pesto.
instructions: Slice focaccia in half horizontally.
Spread pesto on the bottom half.
Layer sliced mozzarella, roasted red peppers, sun-dried tomatoes, and arugula.
Drizzle with olive oil, season with salt and pepper, place top half.
Press gently, slice, and serve.
tips: Lightly toast the focaccia for extra crunch.
Press the sandwich with a weight for a few minutes for a panini-style result.

name: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: Simple Italian pasta with garlic, olive oil, chili flakes, and parsley.
instructions: Cook spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, thinly slice garlic and gently fry in olive oil over low heat until golden (not brown).
Add chili flakes, then add the drained pasta directly to the pan.
Toss with a splash of pasta water to create a silky sauce that coats the noodles.
Finish with parsley and parmesan; serve immediately.
tips: Slice garlic thin and cook on low heat — burnt garlic tastes bitter.
Pasta water is the secret — its starch helps create the sauce.

name: Penne Arrabbiata
description: Penne pasta in a fiery tomato sauce with garlic and red chili.
instructions: Cook penne in salted boiling water until al dente; drain.
Heat olive oil, add sliced garlic and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add canned tomatoes, break them up, season with salt, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Toss the cooked penne in the sauce until well coated.
Serve with torn basil and grated parmesan.
tips: Arrabbiata means angry — adjust chili to your heat preference.
The sauce should be slightly chunky, not smooth.

name: Margherita Pizza
description: Classic Italian pizza with tomato sauce, fresh mozzarella, and basil on a thin crust.
instructions: Crush tomatoes by hand with garlic, salt, and a drizzle of olive oil for the sauce (do not cook).
Stretch pizza dough into a thin round on a floured surface.
Spread tomato sauce evenly, leaving a border for the crust.
Tear mozzarella and distribute over the pizza.
Bake at the highest oven temperature (250+ degrees Celsius) for 8-12 minutes until crust is charred and cheese is bubbly; add fresh basil after baking.
tips: The hottest possible oven gives the best results — use a pizza stone if available.
Less is more — do not overload with toppings.

name: Mushroom Risotto
description: Creamy Italian rice dish slowly cooked with mushrooms, wine, and parmesan.
instructions: Keep broth warm in a saucepan on low heat.
Heat olive oil, sauté mushrooms until golden; set aside. Add butter, cook diced onion and garlic until soft.
Add rice and toast for 2 minutes until edges are translucent. Add wine and stir until absorbed.
Add warm broth one ladle at a time, stirring frequently, allowing each addition to absorb before adding more (about 18-20 minutes total).
Stir in mushrooms, parmesan, and remaining butter; the risotto should be creamy and flow like lava.
tips: Constant stirring releases starch from the rice, creating the signature creaminess.
Risotto should flow when plated — it thickens as it rests.

name: Lasagna
description: Layered Italian pasta bake with meat ragu, bechamel sauce, and mozzarella.
instructions: Make meat ragu: brown ground beef in olive oil, add onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, Italian seasoning, and salt; simmer 20 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, add flour, cook 1 minute, then slowly whisk in milk until thick and smooth.
Layer in a baking dish: ragu, lasagna sheets, bechamel, mozzarella. Repeat 3 times.
Top with remaining bechamel, mozzarella, and parmesan.
Bake at 180 degrees Celsius for 30-35 minutes until bubbly and golden; rest 10 minutes before serving.
tips: Let lasagna rest for 10 minutes after baking — it holds together much better when sliced.
No-boil lasagna sheets simplify the process significantly.

name: Pasta Carbonara
description: Classic Roman pasta with egg, pecorino, black pepper, and guanciale.
instructions: Cook spaghetti in generously salted water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
While pasta cooks, cut guanciale into small strips and render in a pan until crispy and golden.
Whisk egg yolks, whole egg, grated pecorino, and black pepper in a bowl.
Add drained pasta to the guanciale pan (off heat), then quickly toss with the egg-cheese mixture.
Add splashes of pasta water and toss vigorously to create a creamy sauce — the residual heat gently cooks the eggs without scrambling.
tips: Take the pan off the heat before adding the egg mixture — too much heat scrambles the eggs.
Authentic carbonara has no cream — creaminess comes from eggs and cheese.

name: Grilled Salmon
description: Salmon fillet seasoned simply and grilled until crispy skin and flaky flesh.
instructions: Pat salmon fillets dry, rub with olive oil, minced garlic, salt, and pepper.
Heat a grill or grill pan to high heat.
Place salmon skin-side down, cook for 4-5 minutes without moving.
Flip carefully and cook another 3-4 minutes until just opaque in the center.
Serve with lemon wedges and fresh dill.
tips: Do not move the salmon once placed — this allows the skin to crisp properly.
Salmon should be slightly translucent in the center — it continues cooking off heat.

name: Chicken Parmesan
description: Breaded chicken cutlet topped with marinara sauce and melted mozzarella.
instructions: Pound chicken breasts to even thickness, season with salt and pepper.
Set up breading station: flour, beaten egg, then breadcrumbs mixed with parmesan.
Coat each chicken piece in flour, dip in egg, then press into breadcrumb mixture.
Pan-fry in olive oil for 3-4 minutes per side until golden; transfer to a baking dish.
Spoon marinara over each cutlet, top with mozzarella, and bake at 200 degrees Celsius for 10-12 minutes until cheese is bubbly.
tips: Pounding the chicken to even thickness ensures uniform cooking.
Let the fried chicken drain briefly before adding sauce.

name: Beef Stew
description: Hearty slow-cooked beef with potatoes, carrots, and a rich gravy.
instructions: Cut beef into 2-inch cubes, toss with flour, salt, and pepper.
Brown beef in batches in olive oil over high heat; set aside.
Sauté onion, celery, and garlic in the same pot until soft.
Add tomato paste, red wine, broth, thyme, and bay leaf; return beef, bring to a simmer.
Add potatoes and carrots, cover, and cook on low heat for 1.5-2 hours until beef is fork-tender.
tips: Brown the beef in batches without crowding the pan — this builds deep flavor.
Low and slow is the only way — do not rush the cooking.

name: Mushroom Stroganoff
description: Creamy mushroom sauce with sour cream and paprika, served over egg noodles.
instructions: Cook egg noodles according to package directions; drain.
Melt butter, sauté sliced onion until soft, add garlic and mushrooms; cook until mushrooms are golden.
Add paprika and mustard, stir for a minute.
Add broth, simmer for 5 minutes until slightly reduced.
Remove from heat, stir in sour cream, season with salt, and serve over noodles with parsley.
tips: Add sour cream off the heat to prevent curdling.
A mix of mushroom varieties adds more depth of flavor.

name: Garlic Bread
description: Crusty bread slathered with garlic butter and herbs, baked until golden.
instructions: Mix softened butter with minced garlic, chopped parsley, and salt.
Slice bread in half lengthwise.
Spread garlic butter generously on both halves.
Bake at 190 degrees Celsius for 8-10 minutes until edges are golden and butter is bubbling.
Slice and serve warm alongside pasta or soup.
tips: Wrap in foil and bake for soft garlic bread; leave open for crispy.
Freshly minced garlic is far superior to garlic powder.

name: Mashed Potatoes
description: Creamy buttery mashed potatoes — the ultimate comfort side dish.
instructions: Peel and cut potatoes into equal-sized chunks.
Boil in salted water until fork-tender, about 15-18 minutes; drain well.
Return potatoes to the pot, add butter, and mash until smooth.
Gradually add warm milk while mashing until desired consistency is reached.
Season with salt and white pepper; serve immediately.
tips: Use warm milk, not cold — cold milk makes potatoes gluey.
Do not over-mash or use a food processor — it activates starch and makes them gummy.

name: Roasted Vegetables
description: Assorted vegetables roasted with olive oil and herbs until caramelized and tender.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220 degrees Celsius.
Cut all vegetables into similar-sized pieces for even cooking.
Toss with olive oil, minced garlic, Italian seasoning, salt, and pepper.
Spread in a single layer on a baking sheet — do not overcrowd.
Roast for 25-30 minutes, tossing halfway, until edges are caramelized.
tips: Do not overcrowd the pan — vegetables steam instead of roast when too close together.
High heat and a single layer are the secrets to caramelization.

name: Coleslaw
description: Crunchy shredded cabbage and carrot salad in a creamy mayonnaise dressing.
instructions: Finely shred cabbage and grate carrot.
Whisk together mayonnaise, vinegar, sugar, salt, and pepper.
Toss the vegetables with the dressing until evenly coated.
Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before serving for flavors to meld.
tips: A mandoline makes shredding fast and uniform.
Coleslaw tastes better after resting in the fridge for an hour.

name: Hummus
description: Smooth chickpea dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
instructions: Drain chickpeas and rinse; reserve a few for garnish.
Blend tahini and lemon juice together first for 1 minute until lightened.
Add chickpeas, garlic, cumin, and salt; blend until smooth.
With blender running, add ice water to reach a creamy, light consistency.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and topped with reserved chickpeas and paprika.
tips: Blending tahini and lemon first creates an ultra-smooth base.
Ice water makes the hummus lighter and fluffier than room-temperature water.

name: Grilled Chicken Sandwich
description: Juicy grilled chicken breast with lettuce, tomato, and mayo on a toasted bun.
instructions: Pound chicken breast to even thickness, season with salt and pepper.
Brush with olive oil and grill for 5-6 minutes per side until cooked through.
Toast the bun lightly.
Spread mayo on the bun, layer lettuce, chicken, and tomato.
Serve with a side of fries or salad.
tips: Let chicken rest 3 minutes after grilling before placing on the bun.
Pounding to even thickness ensures uniform cooking.

name: BLT Sandwich
description: Classic bacon, lettuce, and tomato sandwich with mayo on toasted bread.
instructions: Cook bacon in a pan until crispy; drain on paper towels.
Toast bread until golden.
Spread mayo on both slices of toast.
Layer lettuce, tomato, and crispy bacon on one slice.
Season tomato with salt and pepper, top with second slice, cut diagonally, and serve.
tips: Cook bacon until very crispy for the best texture contrast.
Use ripe tomatoes in season for the best BLT.

name: Chicken Caesar Wrap
description: Grilled chicken with romaine, parmesan, and Caesar dressing in a flour tortilla.
instructions: Slice grilled chicken into strips.
Chop romaine lettuce and toss with Caesar dressing and croutons.
Place the dressed salad on the tortilla, add chicken strips and shaved parmesan.
Fold the bottom up, then roll tightly from one side.
Cut in half diagonally and serve.
tips: Warm the tortilla slightly for easier rolling.
Wrap tightly in foil for a packed lunch version.

name: Tuna Salad
description: Flaked tuna mixed with mayo, celery, and lemon, served on a bed of greens.
instructions: Drain tuna and flake with a fork.
Mix with mayo, diced celery, minced red onion, lemon juice, mustard, salt, and pepper.
Serve on a bed of mixed greens, in a sandwich, or with crackers.
tips: Use good quality canned tuna for best results.
Add a touch of Dijon mustard for a flavor boost.

name: Veggie Burger
description: Hearty black bean and sweet potato patty with avocado on a toasted bun.
instructions: Bake sweet potato at 200C for 25 min until soft; scoop flesh.
Mash black beans, mix with sweet potato, minced onion, garlic, breadcrumbs, cumin, paprika, and salt.
Shape into 2 patties, refrigerate 15 minutes.
Pan-fry in oil for 4 minutes per side until crispy crust forms.
Serve on toasted bun with sliced avocado, lettuce, and tomato.
tips: The patty mixture must be thick enough to hold shape — add more breadcrumbs if too wet.
Chilling before frying is essential for structure.

name: Pulled Pork Sandwich
description: Slow-cooked shredded pork in tangy BBQ sauce on a soft bun with coleslaw.
instructions: Rub pork shoulder with paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, brown sugar, and salt.
Place in slow cooker, add vinegar and half the BBQ sauce; cook on low for 8 hours or high for 4 hours.
Shred pork with two forks, mix with remaining BBQ sauce and cooking juices.
Pile onto soft buns and top with coleslaw.
tips: Low and slow is key — the collagen breaks down and makes the pork incredibly tender.
Let the meat rest 10 minutes before shredding.

name: Quesadilla
description: Crispy flour tortilla filled with melted cheese, peppers, and onions.
instructions: Sauté diced bell pepper, onion, and jalapeño until soft.
Place one tortilla in a dry pan, sprinkle half the cheese, add the cooked vegetables, then remaining cheese and second tortilla.
Cook on medium heat for 2-3 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy.
Flip carefully and cook the other side until golden.
Cut into wedges and serve with sour cream and salsa.
tips: Low-medium heat gives the cheese time to melt before the tortilla burns.
Press down gently while cooking for even crisping.

name: Burrito Bowl
description: Deconstructed burrito with rice, beans, corn, salsa, and toppings in a bowl.
instructions: Warm black beans with cumin and salt in a small pot.
Mix rice with lime juice.
Assemble bowls: rice base, then beans, corn, shredded lettuce.
Top with sliced avocado, salsa, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
Squeeze extra lime and serve.
tips: Warm all components for a hot bowl, or keep cold for a salad-style version.
Add grilled chicken or tofu for extra protein.

name: Cobb Salad
description: Composed salad with chicken, bacon, egg, avocado, blue cheese, and tomato.
instructions: Chop romaine lettuce and spread on a large platter.
Arrange rows of sliced chicken, crumbled bacon, halved eggs, diced avocado, halved tomatoes, and crumbled blue cheese.
Drizzle vinaigrette over the salad just before serving.
Toss at the table or serve composed.
tips: A Cobb salad is traditionally composed in rows, not tossed.
Use crispy bacon — it adds essential crunch.

name: Turkey Club Sandwich
description: Triple-decker sandwich with turkey, bacon, lettuce, tomato, and mayo.
instructions: Toast all three bread slices until golden.
Spread mayo on one side of each slice.
Layer first slice with turkey and lettuce.
Add second slice, layer with bacon and tomato.
Top with third slice, secure with toothpicks, cut into triangles.
tips: The toothpicks hold the tall sandwich together while cutting.
Use thick-cut bread for a sturdier club.

name: Mac and Cheese
description: Elbow pasta in a rich, creamy cheddar cheese sauce baked until golden on top.
instructions: Cook macaroni until al dente; drain.
Melt butter, add flour, cook for 1 minute; slowly whisk in milk until smooth and thick.
Remove from heat, stir in most of the grated cheddar, mustard, salt, and pepper until melted.
Stir in cooked macaroni, pour into a baking dish, top with remaining cheese and breadcrumbs.
Bake at 190 degrees Celsius for 15-20 minutes until golden and bubbly.
tips: A touch of mustard powder enhances the cheese flavor without tasting like mustard.
Use sharp cheddar for the most flavorful sauce.

name: Grilled Chicken Breast
description: Simply seasoned chicken breast grilled until juicy with a charred exterior.
instructions: Pound chicken breasts to even thickness.
Mix olive oil, garlic powder, paprika, Italian seasoning, salt, pepper, and lemon juice; coat chicken.
Let marinate for 15 minutes.
Grill over high heat for 5-6 minutes per side until internal temperature reaches 74 degrees Celsius.
Rest for 5 minutes before slicing and serving.
tips: Pounding to even thickness is the most important step for juicy chicken.
Always let chicken rest after cooking — cutting immediately releases all the juices.

name: Steak with Vegetables
description: Pan-seared steak cooked to medium-rare, served with roasted seasonal vegetables.
instructions: Bring steaks to room temperature, pat dry, season generously with salt and pepper.
Heat a cast iron pan until smoking hot, add oil, sear steak 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Add butter, garlic, and rosemary; baste the steak with melted butter for 1 minute.
Rest steak for 5 minutes while you roast asparagus and tomatoes at 220C for 10 minutes.
Slice steak against the grain and serve with vegetables.
tips: A smoking hot pan and dry surface are essential for a good sear.
Use a meat thermometer — 52C for medium-rare, 57C for medium.

name: BBQ Ribs
description: Slow-cooked pork ribs glazed with smoky BBQ sauce until fall-off-the-bone tender.
instructions: Mix brown sugar, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, salt, and pepper into a dry rub.
Remove membrane from back of ribs, apply rub generously on all sides.
Wrap tightly in foil, bake at 150 degrees Celsius for 2.5 hours.
Unwrap, brush with BBQ sauce, and bake uncovered at 200C for 15-20 minutes until sticky and caramelized.
Let rest 10 minutes, cut between bones, and serve.
tips: Removing the membrane on the back is essential for tender ribs.
Low and slow in foil first, then high heat for the glaze.

name: Meatloaf
description: Classic American ground beef loaf baked with a sweet ketchup glaze.
instructions: Mix ground beef, breadcrumbs, diced onion, egg, milk, half the ketchup, Worcestershire, garlic powder, salt, and pepper.
Shape into a loaf on a lined baking sheet.
Spread remaining ketchup on top as a glaze.
Bake at 180 degrees Celsius for 45-55 minutes until internal temperature reaches 71C.
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing.
tips: Do not over-mix the meat or it becomes dense and tough.
A free-form loaf on a sheet pan browns better than one in a loaf pan.

name: Chicken Pot Pie
description: Creamy chicken and vegetable filling baked under a flaky golden pastry crust.
instructions: Poach chicken in broth until cooked, shred, and reserve broth.
Dice potato and carrot, boil until just tender.
Melt butter, add onion, cook until soft; add flour and stir 1 minute; slowly add reserved broth and milk.
Add chicken, potatoes, carrots, and peas to the sauce; pour into a baking dish.
Top with puff pastry, brush with egg wash, and bake at 200C for 25-30 minutes until golden.
tips: Vent the pastry with small cuts to let steam escape.
The filling should be thick enough to hold its shape when served.

name: Chili Con Carne
description: Spiced ground beef and bean stew with tomatoes, peppers, and warm spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add diced onion and bell pepper; sauté until soft.
Add garlic and ground beef; break up and brown.
Add chili powder, cumin, and paprika; cook 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes and drained kidney beans; bring to a simmer.
Cook uncovered for 30 minutes until thick. Serve with rice, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
tips: Chili improves significantly after a day — make ahead when possible.
A small square of dark chocolate adds surprising depth to the chili.

name: Fish Tacos
description: Crispy battered fish in warm tortillas with cabbage slaw and chipotle mayo.
instructions: Mix flour, cornstarch, and salt; whisk in beer until smooth batter forms.
Cut fish into strips, dip in batter, and fry in hot oil until golden and crispy.
Shred cabbage finely, toss with lime juice and salt.
Mix mayo with chipotle sauce for the drizzle.
Warm tortillas, fill with fish, cabbage slaw, chipotle mayo, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Use a firm white fish like cod or mahi-mahi that holds up to frying.
Double-layer the corn tortillas to prevent tearing.

name: Shrimp Scampi
description: Sautéed shrimp in a garlic butter and white wine sauce over linguine.
instructions: Cook linguine until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
Heat olive oil and half the butter; sauté minced garlic and chili flakes for 30 seconds.
Add shrimp in a single layer, cook 2 minutes per side until pink.
Add wine and lemon juice, simmer 2 minutes, then add remaining butter.
Toss with pasta and a splash of pasta water; garnish with parsley.
tips: Do not overcook shrimp — they turn rubbery in seconds past being done.
The wine sauce should be silky — pasta water helps emulsify it.

name: Pot Roast
description: Slow-braised beef chuck roast with root vegetables in a rich gravy.
instructions: Season beef with salt and pepper; sear on all sides in a hot Dutch oven with oil until deeply browned.
Remove beef, add onion and garlic; sauté until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 1 minute.
Return beef, add broth and thyme; bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise in oven at 160C for 2 hours.
Add potatoes and carrots, cover, and cook another 45-60 minutes until vegetables are tender and beef falls apart.
tips: Searing the meat deeply on all sides is what gives the gravy its rich flavor.
Do not open the oven frequently — trapped steam is doing the cooking.

name: Cornbread
description: Golden, slightly sweet cornbread baked in a cast iron skillet.
instructions: Preheat oven to 200C with a cast iron skillet inside.
Mix cornmeal, flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt.
Whisk buttermilk, egg, and most of the melted butter; combine with dry ingredients.
Brush hot skillet with remaining butter, pour batter in — it should sizzle.
Bake 18-22 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
tips: Preheating the skillet gives the cornbread a crispy, golden crust.
Do not over-mix — stir just until combined.

name: Baked Beans
description: Slow-cooked navy beans in a sweet and smoky tomato-molasses sauce.
instructions: Preheat oven to 175C.
Mix drained beans with ketchup, molasses, mustard, Worcestershire, paprika, diced onion, and salt.
Pour into a baking dish, cover with foil.
Bake for 45 minutes, remove foil, bake 15 more minutes until bubbly and thickened.
Serve as a side with BBQ meats or sandwiches.
tips: The sauce thickens significantly as it bakes.
Add diced bacon for a traditional smoky variation.

name: Sweet Potato Fries
description: Crispy baked sweet potato fries seasoned with paprika and garlic.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220C.
Cut sweet potatoes into even sticks about 1cm thick.
Toss with olive oil, cornstarch, paprika, garlic powder, and salt.
Spread in a single layer on a parchment-lined baking sheet — do not overcrowd.
Bake 25-30 minutes, flipping halfway, until crispy and golden.
tips: Cornstarch is the secret to crispy baked fries.
A single layer without crowding is essential — use two trays if needed.

name: Garden Salad
description: Fresh mixed greens with cucumber, tomato, and a light vinaigrette.
instructions: Wash and dry mixed greens; place in a bowl.
Slice cucumber, halve tomatoes, thinly slice red onion; add to greens.
Whisk olive oil, vinegar, salt, and pepper into a quick vinaigrette.
Toss salad with dressing just before serving.
tips: Dry greens thoroughly — wet leaves dilute the dressing.
Dress the salad at the last moment to keep it crisp.

name: Buffalo Wings
description: Crispy oven-baked chicken wings tossed in a tangy hot sauce and butter glaze, served with celery and blue cheese dip.
instructions: Pat wings completely dry with paper towels — this is essential for crispiness.
Toss wings with baking powder, salt, pepper, and garlic powder.
Arrange in a single layer on a wire rack over a baking sheet.
Bake at 220 degrees Celsius for 40-45 minutes, flipping halfway, until skin is golden and crispy.
Melt butter, mix with hot sauce, and toss the baked wings in the sauce. Serve with celery sticks and blue cheese dressing.
tips: Baking powder draws moisture out of the skin, making oven-baked wings as crispy as fried ones.
Pat wings bone-dry before seasoning — any moisture prevents crisping.

name: Fried Rice
description: Classic Chinese wok-fried rice with egg, vegetables, and soy sauce.
instructions: Heat oil in a wok over high heat until smoking.
Push oil to sides, scramble eggs in the center, then break into small pieces.
Add garlic, diced carrot, and peas; stir-fry for 2 minutes.
Add cold day-old rice, break up clumps, and toss vigorously on high heat for 3-4 minutes.
Season with soy sauce, sesame oil, salt, and pepper; garnish with chopped spring onion.
tips: Day-old cold rice is essential — fresh rice is too moist and becomes mushy.
High heat and constant tossing prevent the rice from steaming.

name: Kung Pao Chicken
description: Spicy Sichuan stir-fry of chicken, peanuts, and dried chilies in a savory-sweet sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into cubes, marinate with 1 tbsp soy sauce and cornstarch for 15 minutes.
Mix remaining soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, and sesame oil into a sauce.
Heat oil in a wok, fry Sichuan peppercorns and dried chilies until fragrant.
Add chicken and stir-fry on high heat until cooked through, about 4-5 minutes.
Add garlic, ginger, peanuts, and the sauce; toss until glazed, garnish with spring onion.
tips: Toast the peanuts separately for extra crunch.
Sichuan peppercorns give the signature numbing sensation — do not skip them.

name: Sweet and Sour Chicken
description: Crispy battered chicken pieces coated in a tangy pineapple and bell pepper sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, coat in a batter of egg, cornstarch, and salt.
Deep fry in batches until golden and crispy; drain and set aside.
In a clean wok, stir-fry onion and bell pepper for 2 minutes.
Add ketchup, vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and pineapple; cook until sauce thickens.
Toss in the fried chicken, coat quickly, and serve immediately.
tips: Add the chicken to the sauce at the very last moment to keep it crispy.
Double-frying the chicken gives extra crunch.

name: Mapo Tofu
description: Silken tofu in a fiery Sichuan sauce with minced pork and fermented bean paste.
instructions: Cut tofu into 2cm cubes, blanch in salted water for 2 minutes; drain gently.
Heat oil, brown the minced pork until crispy.
Add doubanjiang, garlic, and ginger; stir-fry for 1 minute until fragrant and oil turns red.
Add broth, soy sauce, and tofu; simmer gently for 5 minutes without stirring.
Thicken with cornstarch slurry, sprinkle ground Sichuan peppercorn and spring onion.
tips: Handle tofu gently — it breaks easily.
Blanching tofu in salted water firms it up and removes the raw bean taste.

name: Chow Mein
description: Stir-fried egg noodles with vegetables and soy sauce, tossed in a hot wok.
instructions: Cook egg noodles according to package, drain, and toss with a drizzle of sesame oil.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat, add garlic and stir-fry shredded cabbage and carrot for 2 minutes.
Add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; toss vigorously for 2-3 minutes.
Add bean sprouts and spring onion in the last 30 seconds.
Serve immediately while noodles are hot and slightly charred.
tips: High heat and quick tossing give the signature wok hei (breath of the wok).
Do not overcook bean sprouts — they should stay crunchy.

name: Hot and Sour Soup
description: Tangy and spicy Chinese soup with tofu, mushrooms, and egg ribbons.
instructions: Soak dried mushrooms in hot water for 15 minutes; slice all mushrooms and tofu into strips.
Bring broth to a boil, add mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and tofu; simmer 5 minutes.
Season with soy sauce, rice vinegar, and white pepper.
Stir in cornstarch slurry to thicken, then slowly drizzle beaten egg while stirring gently.
Finish with sesame oil and chili oil; garnish with spring onion.
tips: Add vinegar at the end to preserve its sharp tang.
Drizzle the egg slowly in a thin stream for delicate ribbons.

name: Dan Dan Noodles
description: Sichuan wheat noodles in a spicy, nutty sesame and chili oil sauce with minced pork.
instructions: Cook noodles according to package; drain.
Brown minced pork in oil until crispy.
Mix sesame paste, chili oil, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, garlic, and Sichuan pepper into a sauce.
Divide sauce among bowls, add a splash of noodle cooking water, then top with noodles.
Top with crispy pork, crushed peanuts, and sliced spring onion; mix before eating.
tips: The sauce goes in the bowl first, then hot noodle water loosens it before adding noodles.
Adjust chili oil quantity to your spice tolerance.

name: Steamed Dumplings (Jiaozi)
description: Chinese dumplings filled with pork and cabbage, steamed until translucent.
instructions: Mix flour with boiling water, knead into a smooth dough, rest 30 minutes.
Finely chop cabbage, salt it, squeeze out moisture; mix with pork, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion.
Roll dough into small circles, place a spoonful of filling in center, pleat edges to seal.
Steam dumplings in a lined steamer for 10-12 minutes until wrapper is translucent.
Serve with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chili oil.
tips: Squeeze all water from the cabbage to prevent soggy dumplings.
Using boiling water for the dough makes wrappers more pliable.

name: Teriyaki Chicken
description: Glazed chicken thighs in a sweet and savory soy-mirin sauce, served over rice.
instructions: Score chicken skin lightly, season with salt.
Heat oil in a pan, cook chicken skin-side down for 5-6 minutes until crispy.
Flip and cook 3-4 more minutes until cooked through; remove from pan.
In the same pan, add soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and ginger; simmer until thick and glossy.
Slice chicken, place over rice, drizzle with teriyaki glaze, sprinkle sesame seeds and spring onion.
tips: Cook the sauce until it coats the back of a spoon — it should be thick and glossy.
Scoring the chicken skin prevents it from curling during cooking.

name: Miso Soup
description: Traditional Japanese soup with dashi broth, miso paste, tofu, and wakame seaweed.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a gentle simmer — do not boil.
Soak dried wakame in water for 5 minutes; drain.
Cut tofu into small cubes.
Dissolve miso paste in a ladle of warm dashi, then stir back into the pot.
Add tofu and wakame, warm through for 1 minute; garnish with sliced spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso soup after adding the paste — boiling destroys the beneficial enzymes and dulls the flavor.
Use white miso for a milder flavor, red miso for a deeper, saltier taste.

name: Sushi Bowl
description: Deconstructed sushi with seasoned rice, fresh fish, avocado, and edamame in a bowl.
instructions: Cook sushi rice, then season with rice vinegar, sugar, and a pinch of salt while still warm.
Slice salmon, avocado, and cucumber into thin pieces.
Divide seasoned rice among bowls.
Arrange salmon, avocado, cucumber, and edamame on top.
Tear nori into strips, sprinkle with sesame seeds, serve with soy sauce and pickled ginger.
tips: Fan the rice while seasoning to give it a glossy sheen.
Use the freshest sashimi-grade fish for raw preparations.

name: Ramen
description: Rich pork broth noodle soup with soft-boiled egg, chashu pork, and toppings.
instructions: For chashu: braise pork belly in soy sauce, mirin, garlic, and ginger at 160C for 1.5 hours until tender; slice.
Soft-boil eggs for 6.5 minutes, peel, and marinate in chashu braising liquid for 30 minutes.
Heat broth with remaining braising liquid, garlic, and ginger; simmer 15 minutes, strain.
Cook ramen noodles according to package, drain, and place in bowls.
Ladle hot broth over noodles, top with sliced chashu, halved egg, nori, and spring onion.
tips: A 6.5-minute egg gives a perfectly jammy yolk for ramen.
The broth should be piping hot when poured over the noodles.

name: Gyoza
description: Pan-fried Japanese dumplings with a crispy bottom and juicy pork-cabbage filling.
instructions: Finely chop cabbage, salt, squeeze out water. Mix with pork, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion.
Place a teaspoon of filling in each wrapper, fold in half and pleat one side to seal.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan, arrange gyoza in a circle flat-side down.
Fry until bottoms are golden, about 2 minutes, then add water and cover immediately.
Steam for 5 minutes until water evaporates and bottoms crisp up again; serve with ponzu sauce.
tips: The steam-then-fry technique gives gyoza their signature crispy bottom.
Pleating only one side creates the classic crescent shape.

name: Katsu Curry
description: Crispy breaded chicken cutlet served with Japanese curry sauce over rice.
